source_paper_title: Two Typical Implementable Semismooth* Newton Methods for Generalized Equations Are G-Semismooth Newton Methods
context:
Page 20, Section 6 (Conclusions)

"Accordingly, a natural question is whether an implementable semismooth* Newton method is achievable for solving a GE that cannot be reformulated to locally Lipschitz continuous equations."
title: Implementable semismooth* Newton methods beyond locally Lipschitz reformulations
latex:
Let \(X,Y\) be finite-dimensional real Hilbert spaces. Let \(\Phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) be a set-valued mapping with closed graph, and consider the generalized equation (GE)
\[0 \in \Phi(x).\]
A semismooth* Newton method (in the sense of semismoothness* of \(\Phi\) at a solution \((\bar x,0)\in \mathrm{gph}\,\Phi\)) typically requires, at each iterate \(x\), an “approximation step” that produces a tractable linear(ized) subproblem whose solution defines a Newton direction and yields local superlinear convergence under appropriate regularity.

Problem: characterize and construct classes of generalized equations \(0\in\Phi(x)\) for which there exists an implementable semismooth* Newton method (i.e., with computationally tractable per-iteration subproblems such as linear systems) even when the GE cannot be reformulated locally as a single-valued locally Lipschitz continuous equation \(H(x)=0\).

Equivalently, determine conditions on \(\Phi\) and an associated implementable approximation mechanism (e.g., producing \(V\in \mathcal L(X,Y)\) and residual \(r\in Y\) so that solving \(V\Delta x=-r\) defines a Newton step) that guarantee local (superlinear) convergence to \(\bar x\) without relying on any local Lipschitz single-valued localization of \(\Phi\).
open_problem_quote: "Accordingly, a natural question is whether an implementable semismooth* Newton method is achievable for solving a GE that cannot be reformulated to locally Lipschitz continuous equations."

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
